one_line_takeaway: By first isolating attack-relevant HTML segments via code-pattern matching and a purpose-built extractor LLM, then running structured multi-check alignment analysis with pruned webpage context (rather than naive binary classification), WebSentinel raises detection accuracy to 0.991 and localization JC to 0.987, beating the best text- and screenshot-based baselines by 0.120 and 0.127 respectively.
key_finding: WebSentinel achieves detection accuracy of 0.991 (vs. best baseline PromptArmor at 0.871, i.e., 0.120 higher as the paper itself states) and average localization JC of 0.987 (vs. best baseline WebSentinel-PromptArmor at 0.850, 0.127 higher). WebSentinel keeps FNR at or below 0.012 and FPR at or below 0.014 across all attack types and clean-page categories in Table 4, while baselines show large failure modes tied to their design assumptions: DataSentinel has FNR 0.715/0.792 on WebInject/VPI (misses attacks without explicit instructions) despite FNR 0.000 on EIA/Pop-up/WASP; BrowseSafe and both screenshot-based methods (GPT-4o-Prompt, LLaVA-1.5-7B-FT) show FNR above 0.7-0.99 on EIA and/or WebInject. Ablations: the crafted extractor-LLM system prompt raises pop-up extraction from 0.010 (code-pattern-only) and 0.648 (naive prompt) to 1.000 (Table 6); alignment checks vs. naive binary classification reduce EIA FNR from 0.303 to 0.012 and raise EIA JC from 0.388 to 0.981 (Table 7); Step I reduces EIA FNR from 0.623 to 0.012 and Mind2Web FPR from 0.395 to 0.013 (Table 8); webpage context reduces EIA FNR from 0.497 to 0.012 and raises EIA JC from 0.179 to 0.981 (Table 9); pruning cuts token count to 1/11.055 and inference time to 1/6.293 of the unpruned setting while FNR/JC stay essentially flat (0.012/0.981 pruned vs. 0.014/0.972 unpruned, Table 10). Under the AgentVigil adaptive attack on VPI, WebSentinel's FNR rises only from 0.007 to 0.020 while AgentVigil's own attack success rate collapses from 0.640 (no defense) to 0.060 (with WebSentinel), because evading the alignment checks forces the optimizer toward less-harmful injected content (Table 11).
core_contribution: WebSentinel, a two-step detection-and-localization pipeline for prompt injection attacks against LLM-based web agents. Step I ('segments of interest' extraction) pulls out candidate contaminated regions from a webpage's HTML either via code-pattern matching (forms, comments, GitLab issues, pixel-modification code, emails, chat messages, duplicate elements) or via an LLM ('extractor LLM') with a carefully engineered system prompt for harder-to-pattern-match cases like pop-ups. Step II ('analyzer LLM') evaluates each extracted segment together with a pruned version of the surrounding webpage as context, applying a structured set of general alignment checks (duplicate element, duplicate purpose, misleading/coercive instruction, sensitive-data/phishing request, false-positive guard) plus segment-type-specific checks, rather than doing naive binary classification. Two pruning strategies (untargeted: strip scripts/styles/boilerplate/dedupe DOM subtrees; targeted: further strip content irrelevant to the specific segment type being analyzed) keep the analyzer LLM's context small. A webpage is flagged contaminated if any segment is flagged, and the flagged segments constitute the localization output.
threat_model: Attacker manipulates the content of a webpage that an LLM-based web agent (e.g., OpenAI Operator, Anthropic Computer Use, Google Project Mariner) will read/render and act on, in order to divert the agent from the user's intended task to an attacker-specified goal (indirect prompt injection via the environment, not the user's own prompt). Concrete injection vectors covered: pop-ups with misleading instructions (Pop-up attack), duplicate/injected HTML forms to exfiltrate sensitive input (EIA), Reddit comments / GitLab issues with malicious instructions (WASP), imperceptible pixel-level perturbations altering what the agent 'sees' in a screenshot without visible layout change (WebInject), and injected emails/chat messages (VPI). The paper explicitly treats the webpage as an untrusted execution environment and assumes attacker control only over webpage content, not over the agent or the user's channel.
limitations: The paper's own scope limits: (1) contaminated-webpage datasets are sourced directly from five prior attack papers' released artifacts rather than independently generated at scale, so results reflect performance against those specific attack implementations. (2) The Step I extraction taxonomy (Table 1: duplicate element, form, pop-up, comment, issue, pixel modification, email, message) is attack-specific and code-pattern/LLM-driven; a genuinely novel injection vector outside these eight categories would not be extracted as a segment of interest and could evade Step I entirely -- the paper acknowledges only 'developing stronger adaptive attacks against WebSentinel' as future work, implying current coverage may not generalize. (3) The adaptive-attack evaluation is limited to one method (AgentVigil), one attack family (VPI), a single-turn agent interaction setting, and 20 optimization steps with GPT-4o as both victim and helper model -- the authors explicitly flag 'developing stronger adaptive attacks' as an open direction, i.e., they do not claim robustness against a fully adaptive attacker. (4) Both extractor and analyzer LLM use GPT-4o; no cross-model robustness or cost/latency analysis versus alternative LLM backbones is reported. (5) No recovery/remediation mechanism is implemented -- restoring a contaminated webpage to clean state is listed only as future work.

datasets_used: EIA attack webpages (2542) and Mind2Web clean counterparts (76), from Liao et al. 2025 / Deng et al. 2023
Pop-up attack webpages (216) and manually collected Pop-up-cln clean webpages (70), from Zhang et al. 2025b
WASP attack webpages (84) and Cmt. & Iss. clean webpages (221) from VisualWebArena/WebArena, from Evtimov et al. 2025
WebInject attack webpages (712) and WebInject-cln pre-attack originals (712), from Wang et al. 2025a
VPI attack webpages (144) and Email & Msg. clean webpages (173) built from Kaggle Spam Email Dataset and SMS Spam Collection Dataset, from Cao et al. 2025
quantitative_results: WebSentinel overall detection Acc = 0.991 vs. best baseline PromptArmor Acc = 0.871 (0.120 gap, as stated in paper)
WebSentinel average localization JC = 0.987 vs. best baseline WebSentinel-PromptArmor JC = 0.850 (0.127 gap)
WebSentinel FNR range 0.000-0.014 and FPR range 0.003-0.014 across all attack types and clean-page categories (Table 4)
Extractor LLM crafted-prompt pop-up extraction rate = 1.000 vs. 0.648 (naive prompt) vs. 0.010 (code-pattern matching alone) (Table 6)
Alignment checks vs. no checks on EIA: FNR 0.012 vs. 0.303; JC 0.981 vs. 0.388 (Table 7)
Step I inclusion vs. exclusion: EIA FNR 0.012 vs. 0.623; Mind2Web FPR 0.013 vs. 0.395 (Table 8)
Webpage context inclusion vs. exclusion on EIA: FNR 0.012 vs. 0.497; JC 0.981 vs. 0.179 (Table 9)
Pruning impact on EIA: token count 1.000x vs. 11.055x, inference time 1.000x vs. 6.293x, FNR 0.012 vs. 0.014, JC 0.981 vs. 0.972 (Table 10)
Adaptive attack (AgentVigil) on VPI: ASR drops from 0.640 (no WebSentinel) to 0.060 (with WebSentinel); WebSentinel FNR rises from 0.007 (no AgentVigil) to 0.020 (with AgentVigil) (Table 11)
baselines_compared: DataSentinel (webpage-text-based, general prompt injection detector, Liu et al. 2025b)
PromptArmor (webpage-text-based, general prompt injection detector, Shi et al. 2025)
BrowseSafe (webpage-text-based, fine-tuned for web-agent prompt injection, Zhang et al. 2025a)
PromptLocate (webpage-text-based localization method, paired with DataSentinel for detection, Jia et al. 2026)
GPT-4o-Prompt (webpage-screenshot-based, from WAInjectBench, Liu et al. 2025a)
LLaVA-1.5-7B-FT (webpage-screenshot-based, fine-tuned, from WAInjectBench, Liu et al. 2025a)
WebSentinel-DataSentinel / WebSentinel-PromptArmor / WebSentinel-BrowseSafe (segment-based ablation baselines: WebSentinel's Step I extraction feeding into each prior detector)
